title: Trump administration announces plan for new oil drilling off the coasts of California and Florida
summary: Matthew Daly and Matthew Brown Associated Press WASHINGTON — The Trump administration announced on Thursday, Nov. 18 new oil drilling off the California and Florida coasts for the first time in decades, advancing a project that critics say could harm coastal communities and ecosystems, as President Donald Trump seeks to expand U.S. oil production. The oil industry has been seeking access to new offshore areas, including Southern California and off the coast of Florida, as a way to boost U.S. energy security and jobs. “California is all too familiar with the devastating impacts of oil spills,” responded California Rep. title: US Senate hits stalemate on solution to spiraling health insurance costs

(Photo by Heather Diehl/Getty Images) WASHINGTON — The U.S. Senate in long-anticipated votes failed to advance legislation Thursday that would have addressed the rising cost of health insurance, leaving lawmakers deadlocked on how to curb a surge in premiums expected next year. title: Rare earth trade talks top mining trends: Wood Mac
summary: The shift of rare earth elements to the centre of global trade negotiations is a leading trend in mining, according to a new report by natural resources consultancy Wood Mackenzie. It’s number two on its list of mega-trends in energy and mining this year as governments try to chip away at China’s control of more than 80% of processing and the vast majority of high-performance magnet output. summary: Gulls hunting seeking scraps of fish swarm the docked fishing vessel Gold Rush, which harvests pollock and other groundfish, and Trident Seafood's Kodiak plant on Oct. 3, 2022. (Photo by Yereth Rosen/Alaska Beacon) Lacking the usual amount of data to guide them, federal fishery managers relied on last year’s reports to set the coming year’s harvests for the nation’s top-volume commercial fish species: Alaska pollock . title: Action Alert: Defend the Marine Mammal Protection Act
summary: Defend the Marine Mammal Protection Act WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW Alaska’s Representative Nick Begich recently introduced a proposal to gut the Marine Mammal Protection Act (MMPA). AWA and many Alaskans oppose these proposed changes. It’s important that Alaskans weigh in on this issue. Contact your representatives to ensure your voice is heard: Contact US Representative Nick Begich by email or phone . Contact US Senator Lisa Murkowski by email or phone . Contact US Senator Dan Sullivan by email or phone . BACKGROUND In 1972, Congress responded to overwhelming public support for marine mammals by adopting the Marine Mammal Protection Act (MMPA) . Since that time, this landmark law has protected the country’s dolphins, whales, manatees, seals, sea otters, polar bears, and other much-loved species from harm. Not a single species has disappeared f...

title: AST Warrant Arrest: Wasilla [AK25122781]
summary: On 12/10/2025 at approximately 1951 hours, the Alaska State Troopers conducted a traffic stop on a vehicle near the intersection of Crusey Street and USA Circle. After initially providing false information, the driver was identified as Zayerick Malone (31YOA) of Anchorage. Malone had a $20,000 felony arrest warrant for sexual abuse of a minor. Malone fled from Troopers, crashed his vehicle, and fled on foot. Troopers and Wasilla Police were able to locate Malone nearby and apprehended him with the use of a K9. In addition to the warrant, Malone was arrested for felony eluding, resisting arrest, providing false information, assaulting a law enforcement officer, failure of an operator to give and render assistance after an accident, and not possessing liability insurance. He was remanded at

title: My Turn: Staying connected starts with showing up
summary: When our daughter was 11 and the COVID lockdown was in full swing, she, like many of the preteens and teens around her, turned to the internet for connection. Whether it was bonding over a favorite pop icon or the latest TV show, we watched her find friendship in the only place she could: online.

title: Typhoon Halong: The journey from Kipnuk to Fairbanks
summary: The morning after Typhoon Halong, Patrick Andrews and his family walked out to see whether their house was among the debris in Kipnuk, Alaska. They moved across floating boards, using sticks for balance. (Photo courtesy of Patrick Andrews) This story was originally published by ICT, see the original story here . Patrick Andrews stood outside his home in Kipnuk, Alaska, securing his boats and supplies while the sky above was calm on the morning of Oct. 11. The air was crisp and clear, the horizon stretching endlessly in every direction. An open country where the wind, the rain, and the sea spoke in familiar tones. The lifelong hunter and fisherman heard there was a storm on the way. Typhoon Halong, the forecast said. But no one knew how bad it would get. Weathering storms was part of life in his community. By nightfall, everything changed. “The wind just kept getting stronger,” Andrews recalled, who is Yupik and a member of the Native village of Kipnuk. “It seemed like every 15 minutes,...
